connection: Product holds data. Builder handles construction. Director knows recipes. Three concerns, three classes. The Product never knows HOW it was built.
example: Meal only stores bun, cheese, price. Meal.Builder handles step-by-step assembly and validation. MealRecipes knows how to build a kids meal vs gourmet burger.

connection: Add 'spice level' tomorrow? Add one setter to Builder. Zero existing client code breaks. Old code that never called .spiceLevel() still works -- it just uses the default.
example: 50 services call Meal.Builder. Adding .spiceLevel() changes only the Builder class. All 50 services compile and run unchanged.

connection: Swap VegMealBuilder for NonVegMealBuilder -- the Director works identically. Any builder conforming to the MealBuilder interface can be substituted without breaking the build process.
example: Director.prepareMeal(builder) works with VegBuilder, NonVegBuilder, KetoBuilder -- all honor the same contract.

connection: Don't force a SimpleBurgerBuilder to implement setKidsToySurprise(). Split builder interfaces by what different products actually need: BurgerBuilder, MealComboBuilder, KidsMealBuilder.
example: SimpleBurgerBuilder implements BurgerBuilder (bun, patty, cheese). HappyMealBuilder implements KidsMealBuilder extends BurgerBuilder + setToy().

connection: Director depends on the Builder abstraction, not on ConcreteVegMealBuilder. Add 50 new builders tomorrow -- the Director never changes.
example: Director codes against MealBuilder interface. VegMealBuilder, NonVegMealBuilder, KetoMealBuilder all implement it. Director is decoupled from all concrete builders.
